topic: opinions on AI-generated art in design

turns.content: i need to rant about something. a client just asked me if i could "just use AI" to generate their brand illustrations instead of hiring an illustrator. like as a cost saving measure
Oh, that's a loaded one. What was the context — were they trying to cut the budget for something you'd already scoped with an illustrator, or was this their opening position?

opening position. they saw some midjourney stuff on twitter and were like "can't you just do this?" and honestly i get why they'd think that because some of the output looks incredible at first glance. but like... it's not the same thing at all

You're right that the gap between first impression and actual usability is huge. What specifically concerns you about it for brand work? Is it more about quality control, originality, consistency across applications — or something more fundamental?

all of it honestly. like for branding you need consistency across dozens of touchpoints right. packaging, web, social, print. AI gives you one gorgeous image that looks like nothing else it generates. try getting it to make the same character in the same style on a business card and a billboard and a product label. it can't. there's no system, no design language, just vibes

That's a really precise critique — the lack of systematic consistency. Brand design is fundamentally about repeatable visual systems, and if the tool can't reliably reproduce its own output across contexts, it's not really doing brand work. It's doing one-off image generation.

Did you try to explain that to the client, or was it one of those conversations where the budget concern was really driving everything?

i tried. i showed them examples of how the same prompt gives wildly different results each time. they kind of got it but i could tell they were still thinking "but it's free though." which like, it's not free, my time prompting and fixing and cleaning up the output isn't free. and the legal situation is a mess. you can't trademark AI-generated imagery in most jurisdictions

The trademark angle is actually a really strong practical argument — if they're building a brand, they need protectable assets. That's not a philosophical debate, it's a legal reality. Did that land with them at all?

And you're right about the hidden cost of prompt engineering and cleanup. People see the output and assume the process was instant, but getting something actually usable for production is a whole workflow.

the trademark thing got their attention a bit yeah. but honestly my bigger issue isn't even about this one client. it's the whole vibe shift in the industry. like i see designers on twitter using AI to generate concepts and passing it off as their own ideation process. and some of it looks genuinely good. but there's no craft in it. there's no understanding of why something works, just that it does. like someone who can play piano by ear but can't read sheet music

That's a good analogy. There's a difference between producing a pleasing result and understanding the underlying structure well enough to make deliberate choices. Design is supposed to be intentional — every element has a reason.

But do you think there's any legitimate use for AI tools in a design workflow? Not as a replacement for the work, but as part of the process — mood boards, early exploration, texture generation, that kind of thing?

yeah honestly i'm not a total luddite about it. like for mood boarding and early exploration it's actually pretty incredible. you can generate a hundred visual directions in an hour and use that to have a more informed conversation with a client about what they actually want. i've done that a few times and it works well. it's when people try to use the output as the final deliverable that it falls apart. the last mile of design — the refinement, the system-building, making it work at every size and context — that's where human judgment is still completely necessary

That feels like a very grounded position — use it as a generative brainstorming tool in the divergent phase, but the convergent phase where you're making real decisions about systems and execution still requires a designer who understands why things work.

The mood board use case is actually really compelling because it solves a real problem: clients often can't articulate what they want until they see it, and generating a wide range of visual directions quickly helps bridge that communication gap. That's genuinely useful without threatening the core of what you do.

exactly. like it's a communication tool not a design tool. the problem is the industry is going to collapse that distinction because it's cheaper. and clients like the one today are already thinking of it as a replacement. honestly it keeps me up at night sometimes. not that i need more reasons to be up at night lol

The economic pressure is real and it's fair to worry about it. But the designers who understand systems, who can build consistent visual languages, who know how to take something from concept to production across every touchpoint — that work is genuinely hard to automate because it requires judgment, not just generation. The threat is more to the volume end of the market than the craft end.

That said, worrying about it at 3am isn't going to change the industry trajectory. Focus on the work that AI can't do — the strategic, systematic stuff — and keep being honest with clients about why it matters. That's your best defense.
yeah you're right. honestly i think the rant helped. i just needed to get it out of my system. back to actually designing things with my own hands like some kind of medieval craftsman lol

Medieval craftsman with a Wacom tablet and a subscription to Adobe CC. The romance of handmade work with the reality of monthly software fees. Go make something intentional — that's still the whole point.
